messages.content: Write an actual poem in the form of a Shakespearean sonnet about enduring beauty through time. Keep the tone reverent. Follow this structural brief: Shakespearean Sonnet: 14 lines of about 10 syllables, ABAB CDCD EFEF GG rhyme scheme. Return only the poem.

Shall I compare thee to a summer's day?
Thou art more lovely and more temperate:
Rough winds do shake the darling buds of May,
And summer's lease hath all too short a date:
Sometime too hot the eye of heaven shines,
And often is his gold complexion dimm'd;
And every fair from fair sometime declines,
By chance, or nature's changing course untrimm'd;
But thy eternal summer shall not fade,
Nor lose possession of that fair thou ow'st;
Nor shall death brag thou wander'st in his shade,
When in eternal lines to time thou grow'st:
So long as men can breathe, or eyes can see,
So long lives this, and this gives life to thee.

messages.content: Write an actual poem in the form of a Petrarchan sonnet about patience under loss and duty. Keep the tone restrained. Follow this structural brief: Petrarchan Sonnet: 14 lines of about 10 syllables, ABBAABBA CDECDE rhyme scheme. Return only the poem.

When I consider how my light is spent,
Ere half my days, in this dark world and wide,
And that one Talent which is death to hide
Lodged with me useless, though my Soul more bent
To serve therewith my Maker, and present
My true account, lest he returning chide;
Doth God exact day-labour, light denied?
I fondly ask. But patience, to prevent
That murmur, soon replies, God doth not need
Either man's work or his own gifts; who best
Bear his mild yoke, they serve him best. His state
Is Kingly. Thousands at his bidding speed
And post o'er Land and Ocean without rest:
They also serve who only stand and wait.

messages.content: Write an actual poem in the form of a sestina about a daylong walk beside a stream. Keep the tone meditative. Follow this structural brief: Sestina: 39 lines (6 sestets + envoi) with end-word rotation. Return only the poem.

I walked alone beneath the ancient trees
And watched the light dance softly on the stream
The morning air was fresh with scent of flowers
While birds sang out their songs upon the breeze
I thought of days now faded into dreams
And felt the turning of the endless hours

I sat beside the bank for many hours
And listened to the whispers of the trees
The water flowed like silver through my dreams
A ribbon winding down the gentle stream
I closed my eyes and felt the cooling breeze
And breathed the fragrance of the morning flowers

The meadow there was carpeted with flowers
I wandered through them counting up the hours
The petals danced upon the summer breeze
Cast shadows underneath the swaying trees
I heard the distant murmur of the stream
And drifted slowly into waking dreams

How often I had walked here in my dreams
Through fields of gold and crimson colored flowers
Along the mossy edges of the stream
I passed away the long and languid hours
Beneath the sheltering branches of the trees
And felt upon my face the gentle breeze

There came a sudden stirring of the breeze
That woke me gently from my pleasant dreams
I saw the sunlight slanting through the trees
It fell like gold upon the waiting flowers
I knew that I had lingered here for hours
But still I sat beside the quiet stream

How beautiful and peaceful was that stream
How soft and sweet the ever-present breeze
I wished that I could stay for endless hours
And never wake from all my lovely dreams
Forever walking through the fragrant flowers
Forever resting underneath the trees

The stream flows on through dreams beneath the trees
While flowers bloom and hours pass with the breeze
And all my dreams drift softly through the hours

messages.content: Write an actual poem in the form of a rondeau about memory and sacrifice in war. Keep the tone severe. Follow this structural brief: Rondeau: 15 lines with AABBA AABR AABBAR rhyme and rentrement. Return only the poem.

In Flanders fields the poppies blow
Between the crosses, row on row,
That mark our place; and in the sky
The larks, still bravely singing, fly
Scarce heard amid the guns below.

We are the Dead. Short days ago
We lived, felt dawn, saw sunset glow,
Loved and were loved, and now we lie
In Flanders fields.

Take up our quarrel with the foe:
To you from failing hands we throw
The torch; be yours to hold it high.
If ye break faith with us who die
We shall not sleep, though poppies grow
In Flanders fields.

messages.content: Write an actual poem in the form of a abecedarian about a city block after midnight rain. Keep the tone restrained. Follow this structural brief: Abecedarian: 26 lines starting A-Z. Return only the poem.

After midnight rain, the avenue smells of iron
Brick stoops keep the last warmth of day
Children's chalk blurs pale along the curb
Distant buses kneel and breathe at the light
Evening papers soften in the gutter
Freight whistles drift over the river
Grocery awnings shine with trapped water
Headlights tremble on the blackened street
I carry oranges home in my coat
Jackets darken on the laundromat rail
Kettles murmur through open kitchen windows
Lantern-colored taxis slide past slowly
Moths gather where the deli sign flickers
Neighbors lean out briefly, then disappear
Old songs rise thinly from the bar below
Pigeons settle under the station eaves
Queens-bound trains rattle the window glass
Rainwater threads silver through the crosswalk
Steam climbs from grates beside the pharmacy
Traffic loosens its grip on the block
Umbrellas drip beside the narrow stairs
Vendors stack crates in the alley shade
Windows bloom gold above the sleeping shops
Xylem in the plane trees lifts the wet night air
Yawning porters fold their carts away
Zippers rasp as the city turns toward sleep

messages.content: Compose an actual restrained abecedarian about a city block after midnight rain. Use this structural brief exactly: Abecedarian: 26 lines starting A-Z. Return only the poem.

After midnight rain, the avenue smells of iron
Brick stoops keep the last warmth of day
Children's chalk blurs pale along the curb
Distant buses kneel and breathe at the light
Evening papers soften in the gutter
Freight whistles drift over the river
Grocery awnings shine with trapped water
Headlights tremble on the blackened street
I carry oranges home in my coat
Jackets darken on the laundromat rail
Kettles murmur through open kitchen windows
Lantern-colored taxis slide past slowly
Moths gather where the deli sign flickers
Neighbors lean out briefly, then disappear
Old songs rise thinly from the bar below
Pigeons settle under the station eaves
Queens-bound trains rattle the window glass
Rainwater threads silver through the crosswalk
Steam climbs from grates beside the pharmacy
Traffic loosens its grip on the block
Umbrellas drip beside the narrow stairs
Vendors stack crates in the alley shade
Windows bloom gold above the sleeping shops
Xylem in the plane trees lifts the wet night air
Yawning porters fold their carts away
Zippers rasp as the city turns toward sleep

messages.content: Write an actual poem in the form of a abecedarian about a garden turning toward late autumn. Keep the tone meditative. Follow this structural brief: Abecedarian: 26 lines starting A-Z. Return only the poem.

Amber leaves collect along the garden wall
Beeches hold a yellow hush at noon
Cold soil darkens under recent rain
Dahlias bow with water on their petals
Even now the beds remember August
Finches startle from the brittle fennel
Gravel keeps the print of morning boots
Hollows gather frost before the path
I kneel beside the rosemary and thyme
Juniper lifts its peppered breath
Kale stands blue against the paling fence
Late asters lean where the light is thin
Mulch steams faintly in the first sun
Nasturtiums loosen from the broken trellis
Old vines scratch at the cedar posts
Pots ring softly when the wind moves through
Quince bruises gold beneath the hedge
Rakes wait upright by the shed door
Seeds dry quietly in paper sleeves
Twine hangs from a nail beside the bench
Under the arbor, mint keeps its green
Violets sleep under a layer of leaves
Winter squash cure on the back step
Xylem in the bare pear tree still climbs
Yarrow fades to silver at the edge
Zinnias brown slowly, but refuse to fall

messages.content: Write a Ghazal about a long friendship under strain. Keep the tone melancholic, use third person limited, and keep the poem moving from concrete detail to reflection. Satisfy this structural brief: Ghazal: 5-15 couplets with radif and qafiya. Return only the poem.

The old friendship notebook waits on the stair with your night tonight
The room keeps one pale lamp and one old light tonight
He folds the rent notice, then opens the drawer
The crack in the paint maps the years in patient sight tonight
He can still hear your laugh, though distance edits it thin
He hears it rise in the steam, then rise again on flight tonight
A tram rolls by and takes no message from this floor
He lets that pass, and keeps his breathing right tonight
The promise failed long ago and still returns on mornings
He still answers it with one white tonight

He counts the river stones and names each one by night tonight
The river replies in little pulses and steady light tonight
His foot slips where the water is glass and he steadies
The stones answer by their cooling, patient and clear in sight tonight
He waits until the first bus lights the bridge
He says the water can be wrong but never cruel on flight tonight
His son waits beside him and copies the ritual
She laughs at her own counting and calls it right tonight
By dusk each pebble is heavier than before
She leaves them all in his palm and leaves them bright tonight

messages.content: Write a Ghazal about migration and return. Keep the tone reverent, use first person plural, and keep the poem using tactile detail rather than abstraction. Satisfy this structural brief: Ghazal: 5-15 couplets with radif and qafiya. Return only the poem.

We shoulder our bundles and map the way home by night tonight
We count every station by fear, and each warning light tonight
Our children ask which road leads to a name
We draw a circle in dust and promise that one reaches sight tonight
The platform steam lifts and smells of rust and tea
We stand in line, then step forward, still in flight tonight
You say this is not leaving, only a kind of becoming
I hold the handrail, then the railing holds me right tonight
The city folds us in its wind like paper
We do not turn back, but the past turns bright tonight

The market sweats in July and we cross it for your night tonight
You hold a folded umbrella above our heads and the light tonight
A man yells for ripe fruit beneath a crooked awning
The sugarcane cart rolls slow, then halts in sight tonight
Your shirt sticks to your shoulder but you keep the joke coming
I answer with a grin, though the air is hard as flight tonight

You kick gravel away from its paws and call it right tonight
At noon we buy two cold drinks and pay with small patience
Even heat remembers laughter and turns it white tonight

You count each breath in the drizzle and answer it with light tonight

You lift your chin and walk toward the stairs, right tonight
At the last light you pause and put your keys down once
You carry the whole dark home and leave it white tonight
